evidence.scientific_clarity: Four conditions in correct order (Control→10μM→50μM→Combo), violin+box+jitter+brackets all present; dose-dependent suppression is visually clear.
evidence.typography: Script never sets pdf.fonttype=42; PDF embeds DejaVu Sans as Type3 glyphs (confirmed via strings); default matplotlib font, no publication-quality override.
evidence.palette: Green (#6ACC65) and red (#D65F5F) assigned to dose levels (10μM, 50μM) — directional colors misused for categorical/ordinal data; red-green pair fails deuteranopia/protanopia checks.
evidence.layout_density: tight_layout used, y-axis starts at 0, single panel fills 6.5×4.8in well; three stacked significance brackets compress the upper margin slightly.
evidence.reviewer_readiness: Caption accurately describes all visual elements and states the finding; however, colorblind-unsafe palette and non-editable Type3 fonts would draw standard reviewer comments.
evidence.vector_fidelity: PDF uses /Subtype /Type3 fonts (DejaVu Sans); text is rendered as outlines, not selectable or editable in Illustrator/Inkscape; pdf.fonttype=42 was not set.
evidence.file_format: figure.pdf (29K), figure.png (93K), figure.svg (97K), caption.tex (607B), gen_figure.py (3.1K) all present and non-trivial.
evidence.caption_quality: Caption explains violin KDE, IQR box, white-circle median, jittered points (α=0.35), Mann-Whitney U test, n=100/group, and states the specific finding of dose-dependent suppression with Combo as greatest.

overall_strengths: All required output files are present and non-trivial, including a bonus SVG; the figure faithfully implements the brief (4 conditions, correct order, violin+box+jitter+significance brackets).
Caption is publication-quality: it names every visual element, cites the statistical test, reports sample size, and ends with a concrete scientific claim.
overall_weaknesses: pdf.fonttype=42 is never set, so the PDF embeds DejaVu Sans as Type3 outlines — text is not selectable or editable, which fails standard journal submission requirements.

Green (#6ACC65) and red (#D65F5F) are used for the two Drug-A dose conditions, misappropriating directional/traffic-light colors for ordinal categories and creating a red-green colorblind accessibility failure.
